identity.title: All Other Travel Arrangement and Reservation Services

lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing external ticketing, airline, bus, cruise, lodging, car-rental, restaurant, amusement, sports, and theatrical reservation services, condominium time-share exchange services, and road and travel services through automobile clubs, including transaction processing, customer support, supplier connectivity, changes, refunds, and exception handling.
lens.excluded_activities: Travel agencies, tour operators, convention and visitors bureaus, captive supplier booking functions, software sold without an operated reservation or exchange service, shells, subscale hobby businesses, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include travelers, members, travel suppliers, lodging and restaurant operators, venues, rights holders, and distribution partners. Revenue varies across supplier or traveler commissions, ticketing and service fees, merchant spreads, subscriptions and memberships, referral or advertising revenue, payment fees, and time-share exchange charges; transactions and renewals provide repeat volume, while some automobile-club services include physical roadside fulfillment.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS reports employment and wage shares for parent NAICS 561500 and separately describes travel-agent and reservation work, while industry surveys document current AI use in travel distribution and customer service. Transformation: the parent mix is used to approximate wage weights, then remaining tasks in search, itinerary and offer preparation, customer inquiries, booking support, changes, documentation, marketing, sales administration, fraud triage, and reporting are mapped to AI exposure after discounting mature online booking and rules automation already deployed. Direction of error: the parent's large travel-agency share may not represent this residual code, current digital operators may have less remaining manual work than assumed, and automobile-club roadside operations may reduce exposure; high-touch exceptions and fragmented legacy systems may instead leave more avoidable labor.
a.rationale: The industry's core information flows are structured, digital, repetitive, and language-heavy, making customer support, booking assistance, content, sales preparation, reconciliation, and routine exception handling exposed. BLS explicitly expects online booking and AI to reduce or reshape travel-agent work. Exposure remains below the full workforce because supplier contracting, complex disruptions, fraud decisions, relationship management, regulated payments, platform engineering, and physical roadside delivery need accountable staff.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit time-and-payroll study segmented among ticketing, reservation platforms, time-share exchanges, and automobile-club travel services, with current automation and exception rates separated, would materially change the wage-weighted exposed share.
caveats: BLS publishes the occupation mix for parent NAICS 561500, which includes travel agencies, tour operators, and convention bureaus in addition to this code.
The residual industry combines highly digital reservation services with membership and physical-assistance models, so one wage-weighted task mix is necessarily approximate.
Online booking, rules engines, chatbots, and workflow automation are already mature, making the remaining rather than pre-automation exposure difficult to observe.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: HBX Group reports AI use among its global B2B travel-distribution clients, and Roland Berger surveys global customer-service managers including transportation respondents. Transformation: demonstrated content, customer-interaction, pricing, itinerary, recommendation, and routine-service uses are translated into the share of remaining exposed work a scaled U.S. operator could implement within five years after systems integration, training, trust, process redesign, cybersecurity, payments, and regulatory constraints. Direction of error: vendor-client and large service-center samples may overstate lower-middle-market readiness, while five years of product maturation and strong digital process fit may make current early-stage use understate eventual implementation.
rho.rationale: HBX reports that 65 percent of surveyed distributors already use AI but often only in targeted early-stage applications, while Roland Berger reports broad service-center implementation and travel use cases. Transaction data and digital interaction channels support scaling. Legacy reservation systems, fragmented supplier data, payment and identity risk, hallucination costs, training gaps, and the need for human recovery during disruptions prevent full implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Deployment cohorts from U.S. reservation and ticketing operators measuring eligible contacts, automated resolution, booking accuracy, rework, chargebacks, fraud loss, escalation, integration cost, active usage, and labor hours before and after AI would materially move this estimate.
caveats: HBX surveys its global client base of travel agents, tour operators, and wholesalers rather than a representative U.S. NAICS 561599 sample.
Roland Berger's cross-industry panel included about 90 transportation managers and emphasizes service centers, not the full operating model.
Smaller operators may lack clean inventory, CRM, payment, and policy data needed for reliable automation.

e.rationale: Most listed activities are repeat external services with commissions, fees, memberships, subscriptions, or exchange charges, so a substantial share of LMM firms should meet the default lens. Eligibility is reduced by captive supplier offices, entities misclassified from travel agencies or tour operators, automobile-club operations whose value is inseparable from a broader membership or insurance organization, time-share exchanges with concentrated sponsor relationships, and firms whose technology, inventory access, licenses, or customer accounts cannot transfer independently.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level audit of the frozen population using websites, customer and supplier contracts, GDS and ticketing accreditations, revenue mix, membership obligations, ownership, captive status, change-of-control terms, recurring-customer cohorts, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
caveats: No source measures default-lens eligibility among the 198 frozen LMM-band firms.

The all-other code is intrinsically heterogeneous and may contain establishments whose principal economics are roadside assistance, membership, payments, advertising, or software rather than reservation fees.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged with a broad business-and-consumer-services margin that may not fit merchant, agency, subscription, and membership models equally.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports five-year sale or ownership-transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners, while Carlsquare reports global travel-tech M&A activity that includes acquisitions and growth investments across a wider company set. Transformation: the 22 percent employer intention rate is discounted for plans that do not close, gifts and internal changes that are not qualifying control transfers, platform dependence, supplier-approval risk, and businesses that cannot transfer; observed travel-tech consolidation modestly offsets that discount for scalable technology and distribution assets. Direction of error: intent data overstate completed deals and global travel-tech activity is skewed to venture-backed platforms, but active strategic consolidation may make qualified digital operators more liquid than ordinary small employers.
s5.rationale: Employer succession evidence supports a recurring transfer pool, and travel technology has active strategic and financial buyers seeking distribution, inventory, payments, and AI capabilities. The eligible LMM subset is narrower than the global travel-tech market, and supplier contracts, ticketing credentials, founder relationships, membership structures, or platform concentration can prevent a qualifying transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Completed U.S. control-deal counts and an eligible-firm denominator from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, travel-tech advisers, ticketing and reservation brokers, and lender records, classified by NAICS activity and excluding minority growth investments, would materially change this probability.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers.
Carlsquare's 400-plus travel-tech transactions since 2020 are global, include growth investments, and do not provide an eligible-company denominator.
Automobile-club and time-share exchange entities may have very different transfer patterns from ticketing and reservation platforms.

q.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS describes commissions and service fees in travel intermediation, while Expedia discloses merchant spreads, agency commissions or ticketing fees, advertising revenue, competitive supplier terms, promotions, direct-supplier channels, and AI-agent competition at global platform scale. Transformation: transaction-, commission-, membership-, and subscription-based revenue is treated as allowing initial internal retention of implemented labor benefit, then discounted for supplier renegotiation, customer incentives, search and platform acquisition costs, direct booking, contract renewal, and fee competition over five years. Direction of error: Expedia's scale and concentrated online model may overstate competitive repricing and technology costs for defensible niches, while transparent prices and low switching costs may make smaller intermediaries retain less.
q.rationale: Most revenue is not billed as labor hours, so automation can initially improve unit economics or fund additional volume and service. Retention is constrained by transparent comparison, supplier bargaining, commissions and ticket fees that can reset, promotional spending, direct airline and hotel channels, dominant platforms, and AI agents. Membership and proprietary exchange networks can retain more than commodity ticketing or reservations.
q.change_evidence: Firm cohorts showing commission rates, merchant take rates, service and membership fees, supplier concessions, customer incentives, acquisition costs, renewals, contribution margin, and labor cost before and after automation would materially change retained benefit.
caveats: Neither source measures five-year retained gross benefit for U.S. lower-middle-market NAICS 561599 firms.
Expedia is a global public platform whose scale, brand, marketing costs, and supplier leverage differ sharply from LMM operators.
Merchant, agency, referral, ticket-fee, subscription, membership, and exchange models have materially different pass-through dynamics.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: U.S. Travel Association forecasts inflation-adjusted spending through 2030 for total, leisure, business, domestic, group, and international travel, and BLS projects gradual demand for travel expertise. Transformation: real travel spending growth is used as an activity anchor for the underlying current service basket, with a discount for international volatility, shorter and cheaper trips, ticket and reservation mix, and the fact that spending is not transaction count; elimination or self-service of intermediaries is reserved for o. Direction of error: spending growth may overstate transaction volume when prices or trip complexity rise, while increasing reservation frequency, experiences, and ancillary products could make service demand grow faster.
d5.rationale: U.S. Travel forecasts continued real expansion through 2030 across leisure, business, domestic, and group travel, supporting the underlying need to search, reserve, ticket, exchange, and service trips. International inbound remains volatile and below its prior peak, and consumers are shifting toward shorter or lower-cost trips. The estimate therefore reflects modest real growth in the service basket without assuming that existing intermediaries capture it.
d5.change_evidence: Current constant-dollar revenue and transaction cohorts for U.S. ticketing, reservation, time-share exchange, and automobile-club travel services, including bookings, changes, cancellations, memberships, and product mix through 2031, would materially change this ratio.
caveats: Travel spending is not the same quantity as reservation, ticketing, exchange, or automobile-club service demand.
The published forecast ends in 2030, one year short of a full five-year horizon from 2026.
The code's subsegments are exposed differently to air travel, lodging, restaurants, entertainment, time-share use, and drive travel.

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS describes online-booking substitution, IATA measures passenger preference for direct and mobile airline booking, Booking.com measures willingness and trust regarding AI travel planning, and Expedia identifies direct suppliers and AI agents as competitors. Transformation: commodity search, comparison, itinerary, reservation, ticketing, and first-line support volumes are treated as increasingly direct, self-served, or agent-automated, while complex exceptions, supplier aggregation, payments, fraud, servicing, proprietary inventory, time-share exchange, member assistance, and physical roadside coordination remain operator-required. Direction of error: platform and AI-agent advances may disintermediate intermediaries faster than assumed, while limited trust, fragmented inventory, disruption risk, and regulated transaction accountability may preserve more operator demand.
o.rationale: Direct supplier apps and websites, online self-service, and AI planning are already normal and are expected to strengthen. Booking.com's survey found high future interest in AI travel planning but low full trust and limited comfort with autonomous decisions, leaving room for accountable service. Operators remain useful where they aggregate fragmented supply, control scarce tickets or exchange rights, handle payment and fraud, recover disrupted trips, support members, or coordinate physical assistance.
o.change_evidence: Consumer and supplier transaction panels measuring direct versus intermediary booking, AI-agent completion, human escalation, post-booking service, refunds, fraud, repeat membership, exchange fulfillment, platform traffic, and supplier contract retention by subsegment would materially change this share.
caveats: No source quantifies the year-five operator-required share for the full heterogeneous code.
Consumer surveys are global and often vendor-sponsored; stated willingness to use AI is not completed autonomous booking behavior.
Self-service pressure is much greater in commodity air or hotel reservations than in scarce-event ticketing, time-share exchange, complex disruptions, or roadside membership services.

narrative.executive_view: Other travel arrangement and reservation services combine high digital labor exposure with substantial structural substitution. AI can improve reservation support, content, sales, reconciliation, and exceptions, but direct supplier channels, dominant platforms, and autonomous agents can remove the intermediary rather than merely lower its cost.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can interpret trip requests, search and compare offers, draft itineraries, personalize recommendations, answer routine questions, prepare marketing, summarize cases, detect anomalies, and assist changes or refunds. Payments, fraud, supplier contracts, scarce inventory, severe disruptions, regulated records, complex exchanges, and physical roadside service continue to require systems and accountable staff.
narrative.value_capture: Commissions, ticket and service fees, merchant spreads, memberships, subscriptions, and exchange fees let operators initially retain labor savings. Transparent pricing, supplier renegotiation, promotional spending, customer acquisition costs, direct booking, platform competition, and AI agents progressively share or eliminate those gains.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen population is sizable but not uniform. Transferable digital reservation and ticketing operators can attract strategic buyers, while captive supplier offices, platform-dependent agents, automobile-club units, and sponsor-linked time-share exchanges may not transfer independently. Global travel-tech deal activity confirms buyer interest but is not a U.S. LMM denominator.
narrative.demand_durability: Underlying real travel activity is forecast to grow modestly, sustaining the need to reserve, ticket, exchange, and service travel. The share flowing through the current operator class is less durable because travelers increasingly book directly and use mobile and AI tools. Complex servicing, fragmented supply, memberships, exchange rights, and physical assistance are more defensible than commodity reservations.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are rapid agentic disintermediation, dependence on dominant search or booking platforms, supplier commission compression, cyber and payment fraud, hallucinated travel or policy information, chargebacks, volatile international demand, and an inaccurate mix within the all-other code. An operator without proprietary inventory, recurring membership, differentiated servicing, or supplier access could lose volume faster than automation improves cost.
narrative.principal_driver: Digital booking, support, content, reconciliation, and sales workflows are highly compatible with AI and already show broad industry adoption.
narrative.principal_weakness: The same self-service and AI tools can bypass commodity intermediaries, intensifying direct-supplier and platform competition.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System, United States, 2022
sources.supports: Defines NAICS 561599 and lists time-share exchange, ticket agency, automobile-club road and travel, and airline, car-rental, hotel, and restaurant reservation services, with cross-references excluding travel agencies, tour operators, CVBs, and event organizers.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 561500
sources.supports: Reports employment shares and wages for the parent travel-arrangement industry, including travel agents, customer service, reservation clerks, sales, management, business operations, marketing, and administrative workers.

sources.title: Factors Affecting Occupational Utilization
sources.supports: States that online booking reduces the need for travel agents, AI makes their administrative and marketing work more efficient, and mobile apps and kiosks substitute for reservation and ticket-clerk tasks.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Travel Agents
sources.supports: Describes travel arrangement, reservation, research, cost, documentation, disruption, sales, commission and service-fee work; projects 2 percent employment growth through 2034 while noting online self-booking pressure.

sources.title: HBX Group Report Shows AI Adoption Grows Across Travel Distribution but Scaling Remains a Challenge
sources.supports: Reports AI use by 65 percent of surveyed global travel distributors, positive daily-work impact, targeted content and customer-interaction uses, and scaling barriers in trust, training, usability, and workflow embedding.

sources.title: AI Elevates Customer Service in Travel and Transportation
sources.supports: Reports a global customer-service survey including transportation managers, high use of AI for routine service, travel applications, digital booking preferences, and constraints from regulation, process design, skills, data, and integration.

sources.title: Booking.com Releases the Global AI Sentiment Report
sources.supports: Reports over 37,000 consumers across 33 markets, with 89 percent wanting AI in future travel planning but only 6 percent fully trusting AI and 12 percent comfortable with autonomous AI decisions.

sources.title: IATA 2025 Global Passenger Survey Reveals Mobile and Digital ID as the Future of Travel
sources.supports: Documents increasing smartphone use across the passenger journey, direct-airline and mobile booking preferences, digital payment growth, and continuing cybersecurity and trust requirements.

sources.title: U.S. Travel Forecast
sources.supports: Reports inflation-adjusted U.S. leisure, business, group, and international travel forecasts through 2030, including modest domestic growth and continuing inbound volatility.

sources.title: U.S. Travel Forecast, Spring 2026
sources.supports: Provides the 2019-2030 travel-spending table for total, leisure, business, domestic, transient, group, and international segments.

sources.title: Expedia Group 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Discloses merchant, agency, commission, ticket-fee, advertising, and GDS revenue models and competition from direct suppliers, travel platforms, search, generative AI, AI agents, promotions, and supplier terms.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years and documents the intent-based survey population.

sources.title: Carlsquare TravelTech Market Report, Spring 2025
sources.supports: Reports more than 400 global travel-tech M&A transactions since 2020 with over $27 billion aggregate value and identifies strategic and AI-led consolidation, while disclosing that counts include growth investments.
